behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watching recursion across directory levels. MOCK-MISS: not a single-function check; it appears at watcher-to-pipeline handoff. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: in-process mocks can miss OS watcher semantics for nested paths. No LLM dependency; no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon against a temporary root directory on disk, create a nested subdirectory, write a file inside it, and assert exactly one transformed output appears for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Behavior text targets deduplication logic itself (path + time window), which is a local state/algorithm boundary. MOCK-MISS says Unit is valid because failure can be reproduced without module handoff or I/O. Rejection-gate for higher levels: behavior does not require real filesystem events, OS timing primitives, or model output.
behaviors.test_description: Call the dedupe component directly with the same canonical file path twice within the configured window and assert first is accepted, second rejected; then advance time past window and assert acceptance again.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly names OS multi-event emission for one write. REAL-INFRA: this is an OS watcher behavior boundary. MOCK-HIDE: mocked event streams may not reproduce duplicate/coalesced native event patterns that trigger real failures. No LLM dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real temp directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple native events (e.g., write+rename/append), capture processing logs/counters, and assert one processing execution for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Failure occurs at live OS filesystem lifecycle boundary (delete/recreate path) while watcher runs continuously. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: fake watchers often miss re-subscription edge cases after inode/path replacement. No LLM dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Launch daemon watching a real directory, delete that directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Primary failure boundary is cross-module handoff/order propagation (arrival -> metadata read -> scheduler -> processor), matching IDEA-PROBE integration-handoff escalator. Integration-brake check: (a) no real OS/network/subprocess is strictly required to verify ordering rule if arrivals and timestamps are injected in-process; (b) no AI output; (c) no multi-agent chain; (d) no LLM-involved orchestration. Rejection-gate for System: behavior text specifies ordering policy, not OS-specific watcher correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Wire watcher-event adapter, metadata provider, queue/scheduler, and pipeline in-process; inject simultaneous arrivals with controlled creation timestamps and assert processing call order matches timestamp order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real write-in-progress behavior, event timing, and file readiness semantics at OS/filesystem boundary. MOCK-HIDE: stubs usually present completed writes and hide partial-write races. No LLM dependency.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real directory, start a slow/chunked write to a file, trigger/observe arrival event, assert processing does not start until writer closes/completes, then assert exactly one successful processing afterward.
